hero.subtitle: ahmad.dev / full-stack · open to hire
hero.title: Full-stack developer open to full-time roles — Python, Next.js, and software teams can rely on.
hero.description: Hi, I'm Ahmad — I turn complex problems into software people rely on. Looking for a long-term role where craft, ownership, and team impact matter.

aboutSection.label: profile.sys / decode
aboutSection.title: The engineer
aboutSection.intro: I'm Ahmad Al-Halwany — a full-stack developer who cares about the whole journey: architecture, developer experience, and the people who rely on what we ship.
story: I grew from tinkering with how systems work into owning production APIs, dashboards, and web applications that teams depend on every day.
Today I work across Python backends and Next.js frontends — favoring maintainable code, honest communication, and outcomes that survive beyond the first launch.
I'm seeking a full-time role where ownership, craft, and long-term product impact matter more than quick freelance handoffs.

principles.title: Own the outcome
principles.body: From schema design to UI polish — I follow problems end-to-end instead of throwing them over the wall.

principles.title: Communicate clearly
principles.body: Stakeholders and teammates get plain language, realistic estimates, and visibility while work is in flight.

principles.title: Ship to learn
principles.body: Small, reliable releases beat big-bang demos — I iterate with metrics, feedback, and room to improve.

timeline.label: Join a team building something meaningful

meta.value: AR native · EN B2 · DE A2 → B1

gridItems.title: Building software with teams — not just for clients.
Engineer at heart — product in mind.
Always leveling up — backend depth & system design
Hiring? Let's decode the fit together.

projects.title: plaze-shop ecommerce website
projects.des: Explore products and materials seamlessly and easily with an interface built with next.js
projects.challenge: Reduce inventory errors and improve product browsing
projects.solution: Responsive storefront with structured inventory workflows
projects.overview: Plaze Shop is a production e-commerce storefront for building materials and supplies, built with Next.js to give customers fast product discovery and the business a reliable way to manage inventory online.
projects.challengeDetail: The business needed a modern web presence where customers could explore products and materials without friction, while internal staff struggled with manual stock tracking that led to counting mistakes and slow restocking cycles.
projects.solutionDetail: I delivered a Next.js storefront with clear category navigation, product detail pages, and an internal inventory dashboard. The UI prioritizes readability on mobile and desktop, and the data model keeps stock levels consistent across admin and public views.
keyFeatures: Next.js App Router with optimized images and responsive layouts
Product catalog with search-friendly structure and detail pages
Internal inventory management views for stock updates
Tailwind-based design system aligned with the Plaze brand
Deployed live demo integrated with the real business domain
outcomes: Live production site serving real customers at plaze-shop.de
Single stock source — admin updates sync to the public catalog
21+ screens covering catalog, product detail, and inventory workflows
Mobile-first UX for on-site material lookup on phones and tablets

projects.title: Jobs React Native — Mobile Job Search App
projects.des: Cross-platform job search app with Expo Router — browse popular and nearby roles, keyword search with pagination, tabbed job details, and one-tap apply via JSearch API (RapidAPI).
projects.challenge: Job seekers jump between boards — discovery needs one fast mobile experience
projects.solution: Expo Router app with reusable useFetch hook, JSearch API, and component-driven UI
projects.overview: Jobs React Native centralizes job discovery in one mobile experience built with React Native 0.74, Expo 51, and Expo Router 3.5. Instead of switching between job boards, users browse curated listings on the home screen, filter by job type, search by keyword with pagination, and open full job details with qualifications, responsibilities, and a one-tap apply link — all from a single codebase for iOS and Android.
projects.challengeDetail: Mobile job search often means juggling multiple apps and browser tabs with inconsistent UX. The goal was a production-style React Native app: real third-party API data (not mocked JSON), file-based navigation, reusable data fetching, loading and error states, and a design system — runnable on physical devices via Expo Go without maintaining separate iOS and Android codebases.
projects.solutionDetail: I architected the app around Expo Router file-based routes (/home, /search/:id, /job-details/:id, /all-jobs) and a custom useFetch hook that wraps Axios calls to the JSearch API on RapidAPI (/search and /job-details). Components use co-located .style.js files, shared COLORS/FONT/SIZES/SHADOWS constants, and DM Sans typography via expo-font. Home shows popular (horizontal) and nearby (vertical) job lists; search supports paginated prev/next; job details use tabbed About · Qualifications · Responsibilities with pull-to-refresh and Linking.openURL for apply.
keyFeatures: Expo Router — file-based routes: home, search/:id, job-details/:id, all-jobs
JSearch API (RapidAPI) — live job listings via /search and /job-details endpoints
useFetch hook — reusable axios wrapper with loading, error, and refetch()
Home UX — welcome header, job-type chips (Full-time / Part-time / Contractor), popular + nearby lists
Search — keyword query, paginated results, ActivityIndicator and error fallbacks
Job details — tabbed content, company section, Apply button opens employer URL
Design system — DM Sans fonts, shared theme constants, co-located component styles
Cross-platform — Expo Go on iOS/Android; EAS build path for store deployment
outcomes: 16 documented screens — home, search, pagination, job details tabs, all-jobs list
Single codebase → iOS + Android via Expo (React Native 0.74)
Real API integration — JSearch via RapidAPI, not hardcoded mock data
Open source on GitHub with README, run guide, and MIT license

projects.title: Zeus — AI Prompt Sharing Platform
projects.des: Full-stack community platform to create, tag, search, and share AI prompts — Next.js 13 App Router, MongoDB, Google OAuth via NextAuth, live on Vercel.
projects.challenge: Creators need one place to publish, discover, and reuse AI prompts — not scattered notes
projects.solution: Next.js + MongoDB CRUD app with Google auth, debounced search, and tag filtering
projects.overview: Zeus is an open-source platform where creators draft, organize, and discover AI prompts. Users sign in with Google, publish prompts with category tags, browse a community feed, search by keyword or tag, copy prompt text to clipboard, and manage their library from a personal profile — deployed live at zeus-kappa.vercel.app.
projects.challengeDetail: Prompt libraries scattered across docs and chat threads don't scale for a community product. The app needed persistent storage, authenticated CRUD, public discovery with search, and a polished responsive UI — without over-engineering AI generation into v1 (sharing and discovery first).
projects.solutionDetail: I built a full-stack Next.js 13 App Router app with Mongoose models (User + Prompt), NextAuth.js Google OAuth (auto-register on first sign-in), and REST API routes for prompt CRUD and user posts. The Feed component uses 500ms debounced search across username, tag, and prompt text; tag chips filter instantly. Profile pages support edit/delete with confirmation; public profiles expose any creator's prompts via /profile/[id]. Path aliases (@components, @models) keep imports clean; Tailwind handles responsive nav and skeleton loading states.
keyFeatures: Google OAuth — NextAuth.js session, auto MongoDB user on first sign-in
Prompt CRUD — create, edit, delete with tag + creator ref; copy-to-clipboard
Community feed — homepage lists all prompts with creator avatar and username
Debounced search — filter by username, #tag, or prompt text (500ms)
Tag filtering — click any tag to filter the feed instantly
Profiles — /profile (mine) and /profile/[id] (public); skeleton loader
API layer — /api/prompt, /new, /[id], /users/[id]/posts + NextAuth route
Deployed — Vercel production with MongoDB Atlas + env-based secrets
outcomes: Live demo at zeus-kappa.vercel.app — sign in, create, search, and copy prompts
5 documented UI screens — feed, create, profile, edit, public profile
Full-stack proof — UI → API routes → MongoDB (share_prompt DB) → OAuth
Open source on GitHub — MIT license, README with setup guide

projects.title: AI-Powered 3D Website — T-Shirt Customizer
projects.des: Interactive 3D product configurator — customize shirt color, upload logos, generate DALL·E artwork, and preview on a live React Three Fiber model. Vite + Express + OpenAI.
projects.challenge: Static product pages can't show real-time customization — users need to see designs on a 3D model
projects.solution: React Three Fiber viewer + Valtio state + Express API bridging OpenAI DALL·E
projects.overview: This project turns static merchandising into an immersive 3D customization experience. Visitors pick shirt colors, place logos or full-texture designs, generate unique artwork with DALL·E prompts, and export the final canvas — all in the browser. The stack combines React Three Fiber for real-time GLB rendering, Valtio for reactive global state, Framer Motion for UI polish, and an Express server that proxies OpenAI image generation.
projects.challengeDetail: E-commerce configurators need instant visual feedback: color changes, decal placement, and AI-generated art must map to a 3D model without a page reload. The product also required a split architecture — Vite SPA on port 5173 talking to an Express API on 8080 — with clear separation between 3D rendering, UI state, and paid AI calls secured server-side.
projects.solutionDetail: I built a client/server monorepo: the Vite React app renders shirt_baked.glb via React Three Fiber with Lambert material color easing (maath), chest logo decals, and full-texture overlays driven by Valtio snapshots. The customizer panel tabs Color · File · AI switch between SketchPicker, file upload, and DALL·E prompts; Logo/Stylish filters toggle isLogoTexture vs isFullTexture. Express exposes POST /api/v1/dalle with OPENAI_API_KEY in server/.env, returning base64 images applied directly to the shirt. Intro landing uses Framer Motion; canvas export downloads PNG.
keyFeatures: React Three Fiber — GLB model, orbit camera, @react-three/drei Environment lighting
3 customization layers — base color, logo decal, full-shirt texture (Valtio state)
DALL·E integration — text prompt → Express API → base64 image on 3D shirt
Editor tabs — Color (SketchPicker), File upload, AI prompt picker
Valtio store — intro/customizer modes, instant prop updates to Shirt component
Framer Motion — animated intro hero and panel transitions
Canvas export — download configured design as PNG
Split deploy — Vite frontend on Vercel; Express backend for OpenAI (Render-ready)
outcomes: Live demo at ai-powered-3-d-website.vercel.app — full browser configurator
11 documented UI screens — intro, color, file, AI, filters, 3D views
Realistic e-commerce stack — R3F + Valtio + Express + OpenAI in one repo
Open source on GitHub — MIT license with dual-terminal run guide

projects.title: MyStudyBud — Django Social Discussion Platform
projects.des: Community discussion app — topic-based chat rooms, messaging with edit history, user profiles, activity feed, DRF REST API, and Vercel deployment with Redis sessions.
projects.challenge: Students need focused discussion spaces — not generic social noise
projects.solution: Django 5 app with rooms, topics, messages, custom auth, and DRF API layer
projects.overview: MyStudyBud is a community-driven discussion platform where users create and join rooms organized by topics, exchange messages, and explore activity feeds. Built with Django 5 and Django REST Framework, it features custom email-based authentication, role-based permissions (host-only room edits), message edit history, user profiles with avatars, and a read-only REST API — deployed live on Vercel.
projects.challengeDetail: Study groups need structured spaces: topics, persistent chat rooms, and clear permissions — not a generic blog with comments. The product required a custom User model (email login), ORM relationships across User · Topic · Room · Message, server-rendered templates for fast iteration, a JSON API for external clients, and production deployment with session caching on serverless infrastructure.
projects.solutionDetail: I modeled User (email auth, bio, avatar via Pillow), Topic, Room (host FK, participants M2M), and Message (body + old_body edit history). Views handle room CRUD with host-only delete/update, message edit/delete with owner checks, global search across rooms and messages, topics browser, and activity feed. DRF exposes GET /api/rooms/ and /api/rooms/<id>/ via RoomSerializer. Sessions cache through django-redis + Upstash; CORS enabled for API consumers. vercel.json routes traffic to Django WSGI with SQLite copied to /tmp on Vercel.
keyFeatures: Custom User model — email login, register/logout, profile with avatar upload
Rooms & topics — create, join, search; host-only edit/delete permissions
Messaging — room chat UI, edit with old_body history, owner-only delete
Discovery — global search, topics page, cross-room activity feed
DRF REST API — /api/, /api/rooms/, /api/rooms/<id>/ JSON endpoints
Production — Vercel serverless Django, Redis session cache, collectstatic
Admin panel — Django admin for users, rooms, and messages
Templates + static — responsive HTML/CSS/JS with navbar layout
outcomes: Live demo at django-project-social.vercel.app — register, rooms, chat, profiles
11 documented UI screens — home, auth, room, create, profile, topics, activity
4 core models — User, Topic, Room, Message with role-based permissions
Open source on GitHub — MIT license with migrate + runserver guide

projects.title: ASD Early Detection Platform
projects.des: Full-stack AI platform for early autism screening — facial image analysis with Grad-CAM plus a 12-question behavioral questionnaire, bilingual AR/EN.
projects.challenge: Give families an accessible first screening path — not a black-box label
projects.solution: Dual-path Next.js + FastAPI platform with explainable AI and medical-grade reports
projects.overview: Personal portfolio project — full-stack health-tech platform for early ASD screening via computer vision and a behavioral questionnaire (not client freelance work). Helps caregivers run screening via facial analysis (MobileNetV3 + Grad-CAM) or a 12-question survey — bilingual AR/EN.
projects.challengeDetail: Early autism signs are easy to miss without specialist access, and many AI demos stop at a classification label. The product needed two screening paths (visual + behavioral), explainable outputs caregivers can understand, clear medical disclaimers, and a bilingual UX that works in production — not just in a notebook.
projects.solutionDetail: I built a monorepo with Next.js 15 on Vercel and FastAPI on Hugging Face Spaces. The image pipeline preprocesses 224×224 inputs through a fine-tuned MobileNetV3, returns Grad-CAM heatmaps as base64, and generates structured reports. The questionnaire path scales 12 yes/no answers through a trained neural network (~92% accuracy) with tailored recommendations and WHO/CDC resource links. Docker, health checks, CORS, and Swagger keep the stack deployable and reviewable.
keyFeatures: Next.js 15 App Router — image upload, questionnaire, about, AR/EN i18n with RTL/LTR
FastAPI backend — /predict-image, /predict-questions, /health, Swagger docs
MobileNetV3 (~70%) + Grad-CAM explainability on eyes, forehead, and mouth regions
Neural network questionnaire (~92%) with scaler, reports, and support resources
Medical disclaimer, ResultPanel, ChildInfoForm — privacy-first (in-memory processing)
Monorepo + Docker — Frontend on Vercel, backend on Hugging Face Spaces
outcomes: Live demo at autism-spectrum-six.vercel.app with open GitHub monorepo
2 screening paths — image (~70%) and questionnaire (~92%) with XAI heatmaps
13 UI screens documented — home, detect, results, about, bilingual flows
Production deployment — Vercel frontend + Hugging Face Docker API with CORS

projects.title: Payment Transfer System
projects.des: Full-stack multi-branch money transfer platform — SYP & USD, role-based dashboards, reports, profit tracking, PDF receipts, and bilingual AR/EN UI.
projects.challenge: Multi-branch transfers need RBAC, tax logic, and audit-ready reporting — not spreadsheets
projects.solution: Production-grade Next.js + FastAPI + PostgreSQL with 3 role-specific dashboards
projects.overview: A portfolio-grade fintech demo that simulates a real multi-branch money transfer operation in Syria — handling outgoing and incoming transfers in Syrian pounds (SYP) and US dollars (USD). Three roles (Director, Branch Manager, Employee) each get scoped dashboards for branches, staff, transfers, tax/profit inventory, reports with charts, and PDF receipts — with full Arabic/English support and dark/light themes.
projects.challengeDetail: Branch networks can't run on ad-hoc tools: directors need system-wide visibility and fund allocation, branch managers must see only their branch's employees, profits, and reports, and front-line employees need a fast path to create transfers, track status, and confirm incoming payments. The product also requires tax calculation per branch, CSV exports, audit-friendly filters, JWT-secured APIs, and a demo that works end-to-end in production — not mocked JSON on the frontend.
projects.solutionDetail: I architected a separated stack: Next.js 14 on Vercel talks to FastAPI on Render over HTTPS with JWT (middleware + RoleGuard on the frontend, get_current_user + branch scoping on the backend). PostgreSQL on Neon stores branches, users, transfers, and tax rates. Business logic lives in service layers (branch_profits, transactions, reports) with pytest coverage; the UI uses React Hook Form + Zod, Recharts for analytics, jsPDF for receipts, and a full i18n layer (AR/EN, RTL/LTR). GitHub Actions runs lint + Jest + pytest; AUTO_SEED_DEMO seeds 3 branches, 6 users, and 20 sample transfers for instant demos.
keyFeatures: 3-role RBAC — Director (/dashboard), Branch Manager (/branch-dashboard), Employee (/money-transfer)
Transfer lifecycle — create, outgoing/incoming lists, status workflow, PDF receipt generation
Director ops — branch CRUD, fund allocation, employee management, system settings + backup
Branch manager ops — scoped employees, reports, profit/tax tables, Recharts analytics, CSV export
Multi-currency — SYP & USD with per-branch tax rates, benefited amount, and profit formulas
Security — JWT auth, bcrypt passwords, CORS allowlist, rate-limited login, branch_id scoping
i18n + theming — Arabic/English with RTL/LTR, dark/light mode, guide panels on every major screen
CI/CD + deploy — GitHub Actions, Vercel frontend, Render API, Neon PostgreSQL, Docker Compose locally
outcomes: Live demo at payment-system-portfolio.vercel.app with one-click demo login (director / demo123)
28 UI screens — landing, 3 dashboards, transfers, reports, inventory, settings, receipts
17+ backend test files — auth, transactions, reports, profits, branch scoping
Production stack — Vercel + Render + Neon, not frontend-only mocks

projects.title: VoteChain — Decentralized Voting dApp
projects.des: Full Web3 election platform — immutable votes on Ethereum, candidate/voter media on IPFS, MetaMask integration, and a live results dashboard with role-based admin.
projects.challenge: Centralized voting can be altered — elections need transparent, tamper-proof records
projects.solution: Hybrid Web3 dApp: Solidity smart contract + IPFS storage + Next.js admin & voter UI
projects.overview: Personal portfolio project — Web3 dApp for transparent elections (built outside full-time employment). Votes are counted immutably on Ethereum (Solidity + Hardhat); candidate metadata on IPFS. Next.js frontend with MetaMask, role-based admin, and live results.
projects.challengeDetail: Traditional voting systems rely on a central authority that can alter records. The product needed clear on-chain rules (one vote per registered voter, organizer-only registration), decentralized media storage without expensive on-chain blobs, CORS-safe IPFS uploads from the browser, and a polished UX so non-Web3 users can follow the flow — confirm modals, toasts, loaders, and readable live results.
projects.solutionDetail: I built a full-stack Web3 app: the Create smart contract (Solidity 0.8.9, OpenZeppelin Counters) enforces organizer permissions, voter whitelisting, and single-vote integrity. Next.js API routes (/api/ipfs-upload, /api/ipfs-proxy) handle server-side IPFS uploads and image proxying. VotingContext centralizes Web3 state (Ethers.js v5, Web3Modal, MetaMask). OrganizerGuard mirrors contract rules in the UI. React Dropzone feeds IPFS; live dashboards read vote counts directly from the chain after each transaction.
keyFeatures: Smart contract — setCandidate(), voterRight(), vote() with events and on-chain RBAC
Hybrid storage — votes & permissions on Ethereum; images + JSON on IPFS (Kubo)
Next.js API — server-side IPFS upload + proxy to bypass browser CORS limits
MetaMask flow — wallet connect, transaction signing, full-screen loader during mining
Organizer admin — register candidates/voters with drag-drop photos (OrganizerGuard)
Live dashboard — vote counts, percentage bars, Leading badge, real-time refresh
UX polish — confirmation modal before vote, toast notifications, glassmorphism dark UI
Hardhat toolchain — local chain deploy script, contract ABI wired via constants + context
outcomes: End-to-end dApp — Frontend ↔ Smart Contract ↔ IPFS in one integrated codebase
8 documented screens — hero, live results, admin, voter list, confirm flow, how-it-works
Double protection — require(votingOrganizer) on-chain + OrganizerGuard in React
Open source on GitHub — runnable locally with Hardhat node + IPFS daemon + npm run dev

metrics.label: UI screens in case study gallery

projects.title: Mancala — 3D Kalah Board Game
projects.des: Desktop Mancala (Kalah) built with Java 17 and JavaFX 21 — 3D board, full game rules, Minimax AI with three difficulty levels, sound effects, leaderboard, and Windows .exe packaging.
projects.challenge: Classic board games need polished UX, fair AI, and reliable rule enforcement
projects.solution: MVC JavaFX app — MancalaBoard logic, 3D SubScene view, Minimax + Alpha-Beta AI
projects.overview: Mancala is a portfolio desktop game implementing Kalah rules in a modern dark-themed JavaFX UI. A wooden 3D board (SubScene, PhongMaterial, PerspectiveCamera) sits under a 2D HUD for scores, turn indicator, and status messages. The MVC architecture separates MancalaBoard game logic, GameController orchestration, MainMenuView setup, and MancalaView rendering — with Jackson JSON persistence for players and a programmatic SoundService (no external audio files).
projects.challengeDetail: Board games are deceptively complex: Kalah requires correct sowing paths (skip opponent store), extra turns, captures from empty pits, and automatic end-game collection. The UI must animate stone distribution step-by-step, run AI on a background delay without blocking JavaFX threads, persist a leaderboard across sessions, and ship as a runnable Windows .exe for portfolio reviewers who may not have JDK installed.
projects.solutionDetail: I structured the codebase in layers: MancalaBoard.sow() returns MoveResult with animation steps and deep copy() for AI search; GameController handles pit clicks, animateSteps() at 120ms intervals, and scheduleAiMove() with 500ms delay; MancalaAI uses Minimax + Alpha-Beta at depths 3/6/9 (Easy/Medium/Hard) with store-difference heuristics. PlayerRepository saves to %USERPROFILE%\.mancala\players.json via Jackson. package.bat builds Mancala.exe with jpackage. Fifteen JUnit 5 tests cover board rules and AI move selection.
keyFeatures: JavaFX 3D board — Box pits/stores, Sphere stones, AmbientLight + PointLight, 180° camera rotation
Full Kalah rules — extra turn, capture, skip opponent store, auto collect on game end
Minimax AI — Alpha-Beta pruning, 3 difficulty levels, 25% random moves on Easy
Animated sowing — step-by-step pit updates synced with SOW/CAPTURE/STORE sound effects
Main menu — player registration, Two Players vs Computer, persistent leaderboard TableView
SoundService — 9 procedurally generated WAV tones (CLICK, WIN, LOSE, etc.), toggle ON/OFF
MVC + services — GameSession record, GameController, PlayerRepository, functional callbacks
Windows packaging — Maven + jpackage → Mancala.exe; run.bat for dev via javafx:run
outcomes: 15 JUnit tests — board rules (sow, capture, extra turn) and AI valid-move selection
Shippable .exe — portfolio-ready binary via package.bat, no Java install for end users
Documented 5-screen gallery — menu, registration, gameplay, capture moment, HUD states
Open source on GitHub — Maven Wrapper included, MIT license

projects.title: Storybook Interface — Component Library
projects.des: Isolated UI development for marketing and dashboard components using Storybook — document variants, states, and props without running the full app.
projects.challenge: Reusable UI pieces were hard to review and test inside the full application shell
projects.solution: Storybook workspace with stories per component, controls, and visual states
projects.overview: A Storybook-driven interface layer for building and showcasing React UI components in isolation. Each story covers default, hover, disabled, and edge-case layouts so designers and developers can agree on behavior before integration.
projects.challengeDetail: Shipping UI without a catalog leads to duplicated markup, inconsistent spacing, and regressions that only surface in production pages. The goal was a lightweight sandbox where components could be iterated quickly and shared as living documentation.
projects.solutionDetail: I set up Storybook with organized story files, argTypes for interactive props, and Tailwind-friendly wrappers. Components are grouped by domain (navigation, cards, forms) so new pages compose from tested building blocks instead of one-off CSS.
keyFeatures: Storybook stories for core layout and form components
Interactive controls — tweak props without code changes
Visual states — default, loading, error, empty
Tailwind + React — matches production styling
Living docs — onboarding reference for future pages
outcomes: Faster UI reviews — stakeholders preview components in isolation
Less duplication — shared primitives across marketing screens
Regression-friendly — visual checks before merge

projects.title: Zeus Store — E-commerce Frontend
projects.des: TypeScript storefront companion to the Zeus platform — product browsing, cart flow, and checkout-oriented UI wired to the same design language as the main Zeus app.
projects.challenge: Zeus needed a dedicated shop experience separate from the core app shell
projects.solution: Standalone Next.js store with typed API hooks and responsive product grids
projects.overview: Zeus Store extends the Zeus ecosystem with a focused e-commerce frontend. It emphasizes product discovery, cart management, and checkout-ready layouts while reusing the visual system established in the main Zeus project.
projects.challengeDetail: Splitting commerce from the core app avoids bloating the primary codebase while still requiring consistent branding, typed data contracts, and mobile-first layouts for product listings.
projects.solutionDetail: Built as a TypeScript Next.js app with componentized product cards, filterable grids, and cart state handled on the client with clear separation between presentation and data-fetch layers.
keyFeatures: Product grid with responsive card layout
Cart flow — add, update quantity, summary panel
Shared Zeus design tokens and typography
TypeScript models for products and cart lines
Next.js App Router structure for shop routes
outcomes: Modular shop frontend — deployable apart from Zeus core
Typed interfaces — fewer runtime surprises in cart logic
Portfolio pair — complements the main Zeus case study

projects.title: Chat App — Real-Time Messaging
projects.des: TypeScript chat client with room-based messaging, message history, and a clean conversation UI — practice project for real-time patterns and authenticated sessions.
projects.challenge: Real-time UX requires instant delivery, scroll stability, and clear read states
projects.solution: React chat UI with WebSocket or polling backend integration and optimistic sends
projects.overview: A messaging application focused on the frontend experience of real-time chat: conversation list, active thread, timestamps, and input handling. Structured to plug into Socket.io or similar backends.
projects.challengeDetail: Chat UIs fail when messages jump on scroll, duplicates appear on retry, or connection drops go unhandled. The project targets predictable list virtualization patterns and connection status feedback.
projects.solutionDetail: Componentized layout (sidebar + thread), controlled input with send on Enter, message bubbles grouped by sender, and hooks abstracting the transport layer so the UI stays backend-agnostic.
keyFeatures: Conversation sidebar with active room highlight
Message thread — bubbles, timestamps, auto-scroll
Optimistic send with rollback on failure
Connection status indicator
TypeScript types for users, rooms, and messages
outcomes: Reusable chat UI shell for backend experiments
Documented real-time UX patterns in portfolio
Open source — extensible to group channels or media

projects.title: User Dashboard — Admin Panel
projects.des: TypeScript admin dashboard with overview metrics, tables, and navigation patterns typical of SaaS back-office tools.
projects.challenge: Admin tools need scannable metrics and dense data without clutter
projects.solution: Dashboard layout with sidebar nav, stat cards, and filterable data tables
projects.overview: A user-facing admin dashboard prototype covering the layouts recruiters expect: KPI cards, recent activity, user management table, and settings entry points. Built to demonstrate SPA routing and component composition.
projects.challengeDetail: Dashboards easily become unreadable when every widget competes for attention. The focus was hierarchy — primary metrics up top, drill-down tables below, persistent navigation for common tasks.
projects.solutionDetail: Responsive grid of summary cards, reusable table component with sort/filter placeholders, collapsible sidebar for mobile, and theme-consistent typography using Tailwind utility patterns.
keyFeatures: KPI stat cards with trend placeholders
Data table — users, roles, status columns
Sidebar + top bar navigation shell
Responsive collapse — usable on tablet widths
Modular pages — overview, users, settings stubs
outcomes: Portfolio-ready admin UI patterns
Composable layout reusable in client projects
TypeScript props for table and card widgets

projects.title: Hat & Jacket Detection — YOLOv8
projects.des: Python computer-vision pipeline using YOLOv8 to detect hats and jackets in images — training, inference, and evaluation for custom apparel classes.
projects.challenge: Off-the-shelf detectors miss domain-specific apparel classes
projects.solution: Custom YOLOv8 fine-tuning on labeled hat/jacket datasets with inference scripts
projects.overview: An object-detection project targeting two apparel categories with the Ultralytics YOLOv8 stack. Covers dataset preparation, training configuration, validation metrics, and batch inference on sample images.
projects.challengeDetail: Generic COCO-trained models do not reliably segment hats vs jackets in varied poses and lighting. A small custom dataset and fine-tuned weights were needed for meaningful precision on portfolio demos.
projects.solutionDetail: Organized images into YOLO label format, configured training hyperparameters, tracked mAP on a hold-out set, and exported weights for a simple predict script that draws bounding boxes on new inputs.
keyFeatures: YOLOv8 training pipeline — yaml config, epochs, imgsz
Custom classes — hat and jacket
Validation plots — precision/recall curves
Inference script — single image and folder batch
Requirements pinned — reproducible Python env
outcomes: End-to-end CV workflow documented in repo
Custom weights for apparel-specific detection
Foundation for retail or safety PPE extensions

projects.title: Feature Detection — OpenCV
projects.des: Jupyter notebooks exploring classical feature detectors — Harris corners, SIFT, ORB, and matching pipelines with OpenCV.
projects.challenge: Understanding when classical features beat deep learning on small datasets
projects.solution: Notebook series comparing detectors, descriptors, and brute-force matching
projects.overview: Educational computer-vision notebooks that visualize keypoint detection and descriptor matching across image pairs. Useful for learning geometry-based approaches before jumping to neural detectors.
projects.challengeDetail: Feature detection theory is abstract without side-by-side visual comparisons of repeatability, scale invariance, and match quality under rotation or blur.
projects.solutionDetail: Each notebook isolates one algorithm, plots detected keypoints, runs matcher pipelines, and discusses trade-offs (speed vs accuracy, patented vs free algorithms like ORB).
keyFeatures: Harris corner detection visualization
SIFT / ORB keypoints and descriptors
Brute-force and FLANN matching demos
Homography estimation from good matches
Notebook-friendly OpenCV + NumPy workflow
outcomes: Visual study guide for classical CV interviews
Reusable notebook templates for new image pairs
Clear comparison of detector strengths

projects.title: Machine Learning Project — Experiments
projects.des: Collection of Jupyter notebooks for supervised learning experiments — preprocessing, model training, and evaluation on tabular and sample datasets.
projects.challenge: ML coursework needs reproducible notebooks with clear train/test splits
projects.solution: Structured notebooks with scikit-learn pipelines and metric reporting
projects.overview: A sandbox repository for machine-learning coursework and self-study: load data, engineer features, fit baseline models, and compare metrics. Focus on clarity and reproducibility rather than production deployment.
projects.challengeDetail: Ad-hoc notebooks often leak test data or omit seed fixing, making results impossible to reproduce or compare across model changes.
projects.solutionDetail: Standardized sections — EDA, split, baseline, tuned model, confusion matrix / RMSE — with random seeds and pip requirements noted at the top of each notebook.
keyFeatures: pandas + scikit-learn preprocessing pipelines
Train/validation/test splits with fixed seeds
Classification and regression examples
Metric tables — accuracy, F1, MAE as appropriate
Matplotlib/seaborn plots for error analysis
outcomes: Reproducible ML study artifacts on GitHub
Interview-ready walkthrough of modeling steps
Base for future Kaggle or capstone extensions

projects.title: Outverse — Collaborative Platform
projects.des: TypeScript web app exploring a community-oriented product surface — modular pages, auth-ready routing, and extensible content sections.
projects.challenge: Early-stage community products need flexible UI without over-engineering backend
projects.solution: Next.js frontend with composable sections and typed content models
projects.overview: Outverse is an experimental platform scaffold for a community-focused product. The codebase prioritizes readable routing, shared layout components, and room to grow into feeds, profiles, or spaces without a rewrite.
projects.challengeDetail: Greenfield social products often hard-code pages that do not survive pivoting feature scope. The architecture keeps presentation flexible while data layers remain swappable.
projects.solutionDetail: App Router structure with layout shells, reusable section blocks, TypeScript interfaces for posts/users placeholders, and Tailwind-driven responsive design consistent with other portfolio Next.js work.
keyFeatures: Next.js App Router — layouts and nested routes
Composable landing sections
TypeScript models for users and content stubs
Responsive navigation and footer patterns
Monorepo-friendly module boundaries
outcomes: Extensible scaffold for community features
Demonstrates product-minded frontend structure
Open source — documented README on GitHub

workExperience.title: Full-Stack Payment System — Al Ankabot
workExperience.desc: Led end-to-end development of a secure transaction platform with web and desktop clients.
workExperience.overview: Led the full lifecycle of a secure payments platform — backend APIs, encrypted auth, Next.js web app, and a PyQt desktop client — built for reliability at scale with multi-layer security.
responsibilities: Architected FastAPI backend with JWT authentication and encryption for sensitive financial flows
Built Next.js web interface and PyQt desktop client sharing the same transaction core
Implemented multi-stage security protocols to mitigate data-leak and abuse scenarios
Monitored uptime and performance for production traffic exceeding 1,000 transactions per month
outcomes: 1,000+ transactions processed monthly at 99.9% system availability
Secure, auditable payment flows with layered protection against data leaks

workExperience.title: Frontend Developer — LIT-Co

workExperience.overview: Drove frontend and platform work at LIT-Co — owning the Plaze e-commerce build, WordPress customizations, and a full site redesign that materially improved mobile and conversion metrics.
responsibilities: Led development of the Plaze e-commerce platform with Next.js and GraphQL, raising conversion rate by 15%
Customized 10+ WordPress themes and plugins, increasing user interaction by 20%
Led a comprehensive website redesign that improved responsiveness and grew mobile traffic by 40%

outcomes: Plaze shop live as a production e-commerce storefront at plaze-shop.de
Measurable gains in conversion, engagement, and mobile reach

workExperience.title: Frontend Developer Intern — LIT-Co

workExperience.overview: Started at LIT-Co on the frontend team — shipping UI components, fixing bugs, and learning production Next.js practices before moving into the Frontend Developer role.
responsibilities: Built and refined React/Next.js UI components under senior developer guidance
Improved interactivity and client-side flows on the company web platform
Participated in code reviews, stand-ups, and incremental feature delivery
Supported WordPress and frontend tasks that prepared me for leading Plaze development
